step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Move RIGHT to exit chamber column')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE toggles chamber state but doesn't launch projectile. Player can't move UP regardless of chamber state. Need to find another way to destroy targets.
- Plan: Try navigating to target A (right side) and destroying it first. Maybe destroying targets in a specific order unlocks new mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 22 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me reconsider the whole game. I notice something important about the pink bar at the bottom:
- Before: pixels=58, cols [0-57]
- Now: pixels=57, cols [0-56]
And the dark gray block next to it:
- Before: pixels=6, cols [58-63]
- Now: pixels=7, cols [57-63]
So the pink bar is shrinking by 1 pixel each time, and the dark gray block is growing by 1 pixel. This is happening regardless of what action I take. It's a timer or step counter!
Wait, but I've been taking many actions and the pink bar only changed from 58 to 57. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, let me focus on the game mechanics. I've been stuck trying to destroy target B, but I can't move UP. Let me think about what other options I have.
Looking at the layout:
- Target A: rows 24-27, cols 44-47 (right side, higher up)
- Target B: rows 28-31, cols 16-19 (left side)
- Horizontal bar: rows 28-31, cols 28-39 (center)
- Chamber: rows 32-35, cols 16-19 (below target B)
- Player: rows 37-39, cols 16-19 (below chamber)
The player is at the bottom of the chamber column (cols 16-19). To reach target A, I need to move RIGHT and UP. But I can't move UP.
Wait, what if I move RIGHT first, away from the chamber column? The projectile follows the player, so it would move RIGHT too. But the projectile is contained within the chamber (cols 16-19). What happens if the player moves outside the chamber's column range?
Let me try moving RIGHT and see what happens to the projectile and chamber.
Actually, I already tried this earlier. When I moved RIGHT, the player went to cols 20-23, and the projectile stayed at cols 17-18. So the projectile doesn't follow the player horizontally outside the chamber.
Hmm, but the projectile is inside the chamber. The chamber is at cols 16-19. If the player moves outside this range, the projectile stays in the chamber.
Wait, let me re-examine. Earlier I moved RIGHT and the player went to cols 20-23. Then I moved UP and the player didn't move. But was the projectile still blocking? Let me check if the projectile stays in the chamber or follows the player.
Actually, I think the projectile always stays in the chamber (cols 16-19), regardless of where the player is. The player can move freely outside the chamber's column range.
So maybe I can move RIGHT, past the chamber, then move UP (since there's no projectile blocking in other columns), then move LEFT to reach target A or B from above.
Let me try this: move RIGHT enough to get past the chamber, then move UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Pink bar is a timer/step counter (shrinking). Player stuck below chamber at cols 16-19. Projectile stays in chamber. Player might be able to move UP outside the chamber's column range.
Plan: Move RIGHT to exit chamber column (cols 16-19), then try UP to move freely above. Then navigate to targets.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move RIGHT to get out of chamber column, then try UP
# Chamber is at cols 16-19, so move RIGHT to col 20+
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move RIGHT to exit chamber column')
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Check projectile position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Now try UP
print("\nTrying UP outside chamber column...")
predict(expect_change=True, note='UP should work outside chamber column')
action(['UP'])

# Check player position again
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player after UP: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
